Furthermore, His saying regarding him: كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِ أَنَّهُ مَن تَوَلَّاهُ فَأَنَّهُ يُضِلُّهُ — "It has been ordained for him that whoever takes him for a friend, he will mislead him" (Al-Hajj: 4) and His saying: إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا الشَّيَاطِينَ أَوْلِيَاءَ لِلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ — "Indeed We have made the devils friends of those who do not have faith" (Al-A'raf: 27) explicitly indicate the establishment of his authority and that it is by the placement and decree of Allah, the Glorified.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمَوْعِدُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ — "Indeed hell is the tryst of them all" (Al-Hijr: 43). It appears that "tryst" (موعد) is a noun of place (اسم مكان), and the meaning of Hell being their tryst is that it is the place for the fulfillment of the punishment Allah promised them.

This is a confirmation from the Glorified of the establishment of His power and that the entire matter returns to Him. It is as if the Exalted is saying to him: "The authority you mentioned over the perverse is not from yourself, nor have you frustrated Us; rather, We gave you power over them because of their following you, and furthermore, We shall soon recompense them with the punishment of Hell."

Because the speech is intended to explain their condition, He restricted it to mentioning their recompense and did not mention Iblis or his recompense with them, unlike His saying: لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنكَ وَمِمَّن تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ — "I will surely fill hell with you and with those of them who follow you, all together" (Sad: 85) and His saying: فَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ جَزَاؤُكُمْ جَزَاءً مَّوْفُورًا — "then hell shall be your recompense, an ample recompense" (Al-Isra: 63), because the context here is different.

His saying, the Exalted: لَهَا سَبْعَةُ أَبْوَابٍ لِّكُلِّ بَابٍ مِّنْهُمْ جُزْءٌ مَّقْسُومٌ — "It has seven gates, and to each gate belongs a separate portion of them" (Al-Hijr: 44). The Glorified has not clarified in any of His explicit speech what is meant by these "gates" (أبواب). Are they like the gates of walls—entrances that lead everyone to a single courtyard? Or are they levels (طبقات) and degrees (دركات) that differ in the type and intensity of punishment?

Often, in matters of various types, each type is called a "gate" (باب), as it is said: the gates of good, the gates of evil, and the gates of mercy. The Exalted said: فَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ أَبْوَابَ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "We opened for them the gates of all things" (Al-An'am: 44). Sometimes the causes (أسباب) of a thing and the paths to reaching it are called gates, such as the "gates of sustenance" for various types of earnings and transactions.

It is not far-fetched that the second meaning is derived from various verses regarding the Fire, such as His saying: وَسِيقَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِلَىٰ جَهَنَّمَ زُمَرًا ۖ حَتَّىٰ إِذَا جَاءُوهَا فُتِحَتْ أَبْوَابُهَا — "The faithless will be driven to hell in throngs. When they reach it, its gates will be opened..." until He says: قِيلَ ادْخُلُوا أَبْوَابَ جَهَنَّمَ خَالِدِينَ فِيهَا — "It will be said, ‘Enter the gates of hell to remain in it [forever]’" (Al-Zumar: 71-72), and His saying: إِنَّ الْمُنَافِقِينَ فِي الدَّرْكِ الْأَسْفَلِ مِنَ النَّارِ — "Indeed the hypocrites will be in the lowest reach of the Fire" (Al-Nisa: 145), among other verses.

This is supported by His saying: "to each gate belongs a separate portion of them," for its apparent meaning is that the "portion" (الجزء) itself is divided and distributed among the gates. This only suits "gate" in the sense of a level (طبقة), rather than "gate" in the sense of an entrance. As for the interpretation by some that the "separate portion" refers to a specific group distinguished from others, its weakness is apparent.

Accordingly, Hell having seven gates means that the punishment prepared therein is diversified into seven types, and then each type is divided into sections according to the division of the portion that enters and remains in it. This necessitates the division of the sins that cause entry into it into seven categories, as well as the division of the paths leading to and the causes inviting those sins into that same division. By this, the narrations (الروايات) mentioned regarding these meanings are supported, as will come to you, if Allah wills.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَعُيُونٍ ادْخُلُوها بِسَلامٍ آمِنِينَ — "Indeed the God-conscious will be amid gardens and springs. ‘Enter them in peace and safety!’" (Al-Hijr: 45-46). That is, they are established in gardens and springs, and it is said to them: "Enter them with a peace (سلام) that cannot be described or fully grasped, while you are secure (آمنين) from every evil and harm."

When the Glorified mentioned His decree regarding those who follow Iblis of the perverse, He mentioned what He decreed for the God-conscious (المتقين) regarding Paradise. The interpretation of godwariness (التقوى) — the protective shield of piety and restraint from sin — has been mentioned in the speech of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) as refraining from the prohibitions of Allah. Since their glad tidings of Paradise are repeated in the speech of the Exalted, the God-conscious are more general than the purified (المخلصين).

As for what has been said—that there is no doubt the context indicates the God-conscious are the purified ones mentioned previously, and that the absolute (المطلق) is carried upon the perfect individual (الفرد الكامل)—

The response is that this is based on the assumption that "My servants" in His saying: "Indeed, as for My servants, you shall have no authority over them" refers only to the purified, such that the context is restricted to them. However, it has preceded that "My servants" refers to the general members of humanity; the perverse were excluded by the exception, and the rest remained. The Glorified mentioned His decree for the perverse as being the Fire, and here He mentions His decree for others among those for whom He has made Paradise mandatory. The matter of the oppressed (المستضعفون) is deferred, and the matter of the sinners among the people of major sins who die without repentance is linked to intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah. Thus, the people of godwariness among the believers remain, and they are more general than the purified; so Paradise was decreed for them.

As for the notion of carrying the absolute (المطلق) upon the perfect individual (الفرد الكامل), it is an error; rather, it is carried upon the common individual (الفرد المتعارف), and the detail of this matter is in the science of principles (فن الأصول).

Imam al-Razi mentioned in his Tafsir that the "God-conscious" in the verse refers to those who avoided polytheism (الشرك), attributing this to the majority of the Companions and Successors and basing it on a report.

He said: "This is the correct truth. What indicates this is that the 'God-conscious' (المتقي) is one who performs an act of godwariness (التقوى) even once, just as the 'striker' (الضارب) is one who performs the act of striking once. It is not a condition for the truth of the description of being God-conscious that one performs all types of godwariness. What establishes this is that one who performs a single instance of godwariness is performing godwariness, for the individual instance necessarily contains the essence (الماهية). Every performer of godwariness must be God-conscious; therefore, the performer of a single instance must be God-conscious. For this reason, they said: the apparent command does not imply repetition. Thus, the literal sense of the verse necessitates the attainment of gardens and springs for everyone who has guarded against even a single sin, except that the Ummah is in consensus that guarding against disbelief (الكفر) is a condition for the attainment of this ruling.

Furthermore, this verse came after the saying of Iblis: 'except Your servants among them, the purified,' and after the saying of the Exalted: 'Indeed, as for My servants, you shall have no authority over them.' Therefore, faith (الإيمان) was considered in this ruling, and it is mandatory that no other restriction be added. Since the specification (تخصيص) of a general term (العام) is contrary to the literal sense (الظاهر), the less the specification, the more it accords with the original principle (الأصل) and the literal sense.

Thus, it is established that the mentioned ruling covers all those who say: 'There is no god but Allah, Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah,' even if they are people of disobedience. This is a clear demonstration and obvious speech." End quote.

The implication of his speech is the inclusion in the verse of anyone who avoided polytheism, even if he committed all the destructive major sins (الكبائر الموبقة) for which the Mighty Book explicitly states the deserving of the Fire, and abandoned all the obligations for which it explicitly states the same for their abandonment. One who is familiar with His speech, the Exalted, and reflects upon His verses, will not doubt that the Quran does not call such a person "God-conscious" (متقياً), nor does it count him among the God-conscious. The Quran has frequently mentioned the God-conscious and given them explicit glad tidings of Paradise in nearly twenty places, describing them in many of those places as avoiding prohibitions; and by this, godwariness (التقوى) was interpreted in the hadith, as preceded.

Furthermore, the mere correctness of applying a description is one thing, and naming (التسمية) is another. One is not named among the believers (المؤمنين), the doers of good (المحسنين), the obedient (القانتين), the purified (المخلصين), or the patient (الصابرين)—especially in descriptions that imply permanence and continuity—except one in whom the description has become established. If what he mentioned regarding the God-conscious were correct, the same would apply to the wrongdoers (الظالمين), the transgressors (الفاسقين), the corrupters (المفسدين), the criminals (المجرمين), the perverse (الغاوين), and the astray (الضالين), whom Allah has threatened with the Fire. This would lead to a strange contradiction and disorder in His speech, the Exalted. If it were said: "There is that which diverts these verses from including a single or double instance, such as the verses of repentance and intercession and their likes," then there is also that which diverts this verse from including the one who is God-conscious only once or twice, namely the very verses of threat for the destructive major sins, such as the verses regarding adultery, wrongful killing, usury, consuming the orphan's property, and their likes.

Moreover, the points he mentioned to approximate the indication are weak arguments, such as his saying: "This verse occurred after the saying of Iblis..." for you have already learned that this does not benefit him at all. And his saying: "Adding another restriction after faith is contrary to the principle..." for the principle is only relied upon in the absence of verbal evidence (الدليل اللفظي), and you have learned that there are numerous verses suitable for restriction (التقييد).

And his saying: "That is contrary to the literal sense," by which he seemingly means the literal sense of the absolute (المطلق) in its absolute application. He has overlooked that the literal sense of the absolute is only a proof when there is nothing suitable for restriction.

The truth is that the verse only includes those in whom the trait (ملكة) of godwariness has become established, which is refraining from the prohibitions of Allah. Those are the ones for whom happiness and Paradise are decreed by a necessary decree. Indeed, what is benefited from the Book and the Sunnah is that the people of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — who attend the Station with the testimony that there is no god but Allah, will not remain forever in the Fire and will inevitably enter Paradise. But this is different from the indication of the verse of the God-conscious regarding that.

His saying, the Exalted: وَنَزَعْنَا مَا فِي صُدُورِهِم مِّنْ غِلٍّ إِخْوَانًا عَلَىٰ سُرُرٍ مُّتَقَابِلِينَ — "We will remove whatever rancour there is in their breasts; [they will be] brothers, [reclining] on couches, facing one another" (Al-Hijr: 47) until the end of the two verses. Rancour (الغل) is malice (الحقد). It is said to be the malice and envy in the breast that drives a person to harm others. Couches (السرر) is the plural of sarir. Fatigue (النصب) is the tiredness and exhaustion that comes from outside.

In these two verses, the Exalted describes the state of the God-conscious in their happiness upon entering Paradise. He specifically mentioned these matters among the blessings of Paradise, despite their abundance, because the concern necessitated by the context is related to explaining that they are in peace (سلام) and safety (أمن) from what the perverse were afflicted with—the nullification of happiness and the loss of sovereignty and dignity. Thus, He mentioned that they are in safety from within themselves, because Allah removed whatever rancour was in their breasts, so none of them intends evil for his companion; rather, they are brothers on couches facing one another. Their "facing one another" has a meaning that will come in the narrational discussion, if Allah wills. They are in safety from the side of external causes and factors, so no fatigue touches them at all. And they are in safety and peace from the side of their Lord, for they shall never be expelled from Paradise. Thus, they have happiness and dignity from every direction; no wretchedness or weakness overshadows or touches them from any side at all—neither from themselves, nor from the rest of what Allah created, nor from their Lord.

(Commentary)

The decrees (الأقضية) that were issued from the Source of Might at the beginning of the creation of man, according to what occurred in His speech, the Glorified, are ten:

The first and second: His saying to Iblis: فَاخْرُجْ مِنْهَا فَإِنَّكَ رَجِيمٌ وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ اللَّعْنَةَ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ الدِّينِ — "‘Then get out of it, for you are indeed an outcast, and indeed the curse shall be upon you until the Day of Retribution’" (Al-Hijr: 34-35). These two can be reduced to one.

The third: His saying, the Glorified, to him: فَإِنَّكَ مِنَ الْمُنظَرِينَ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ الْوَقْتِ الْمَعْلُومِ — "‘You are indeed among the reprieved until the day of the known time’" (Al-Hijr: 37-38).

The fourth, fifth, and sixth: His saying to him: إِنَّ عِبَادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغَاوِينَ وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمَوْعِدُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ — "‘As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you. Indeed hell is the tryst of them all’" (Al-Hijr: 42-43).

The seventh and eighth: His saying, the Glorified, to Adam and those with him: اهْبِطُوا بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ ۖ وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتَاعٌ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ — "‘Get down, being enemies of one another! On the earth shall be your abode and sustenance for a time’" (Al-Baqarah: 36).

The ninth and tenth: His saying to them: اهْبِطُوا مِنْهَا جَمِيعًا ۖ فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّي هُدًى فَمَن تَبِعَ هُدَايَ فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَكَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا أُولَٰئِكَ أَصْحَابُ النَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَالِدُونَ — "‘Get down from it, all together! Yet, should any guidance come to you from Me, those who follow My guidance shall have no fear, nor shall they grieve. But those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever]’" (Al-Baqarah: 38-39).

There are subsidiary decrees resulting from these original decrees that the reflecting researcher will encounter.

(Narrational Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: I asked him about the saying of Allah: وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِي (and breathed into him of My spirit). He said: روح خلقها الله فنفخ في آدم منها — "A spirit that Allah created, then He breathed into Adam from it."

In the same source, from Abu Basir, from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: فَإِذَا سَوَّيْتُهُ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِي (So when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My spirit). He said: خلق خلقا وخلق روحا ثم أمر الملك فنفخ فيه ـ وليست بالتي نقصت من الله شيئا ـ هي من قدرته تبارك وتعالى — "He created a creation and created a spirit, then He commanded the angel and he breathed into him—and it is not something that diminished anything from Allah—it is from His power, Blessed and Exalted is He."

In the same source, and in a narration of Sama'ah from him (peace be upon him): خلق آدم ونفخ فيه — "He created Adam and breathed into him." I asked him about the spirit, and he said: هي قدرته من الملكوت — "It is His power from the celestial realm (الملكوت)."

I say: That is, it is His active power emanating from His essential power and issued from it, as indicated by the previous report.

In al-Ma'ani, with its chain to Muhammad ibn Muslim, who said: I asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِي. He said: روح اختاره واصطفاه وخلقه وأضافه إلى نفسه ـ وفضله على جميع الأرواح فأمر فنفخ منه في آدم — "A spirit He chose, selected, created, and attributed to Himself—and He preferred it over all spirits, so He commanded and it was breathed from it into Adam."

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Muhammad ibn Muslim, who said: I asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) about what they narrate—that Allah created Adam in His image. He said: هي على صورة مخلوقة محدثة اصطفاها الله ـ واختارها على سائر الصور المختلفة فأضافها إلى نفسه ـ كما أضاف الكعبة إلى نفسه فقال : بَيْتِيَ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِي — "It is an image that is created and originated, which Allah selected and chose over all other various images, so He attributed it to Himself—just as He attributed the Kaaba to Himself and said: 'My House' (بَيْتِيَ) and 'I breathed into him of My spirit' (وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِي)."

I say: These narrations are among the finest narrations regarding the meaning of the spirit (الروح), containing many insights. We will clarify their meaning when discussing the reality of the spirit, if Allah wills.

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Ibn Babawayh with his chain to Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنَّكَ مِنَ الْمُنظَرِينَ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ الْوَقْتِ الْمَعْلُومِ. He said: يوم ينفخ في الصور نفخة واحدة ـ فيموت إبليس ما بين النفخة الأولى والثانية — "The day the Trumpet is blown with a single blast—then Iblis will die between the first and second blasts."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Wahb ibn Jami', and in Tafsir al-Burhan, from Sharaf al-Din al-Najafi, omitting the chain from Wahb—and the wording is from the latter—from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: I asked him about Iblis and his saying: رَبِّ فَأَنظِرْنِي إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ ـ قَالَ فَإِنَّكَ مِنَ الْمُنظَرِينَ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ الْوَقْتِ الْمَعْلُومِ. Which day is it? He said: يا وهب أتحسب أنه يوم يبعث الله فيه الناس ـ ولكن الله عز وجل أنظره إلى يوم يبعث فيه قائمنا ـ فيأخذ بناصيته ويضرب عنقه فذلك اليوم هو الوقت المعلوم — "O Wahb, do you think it is the day Allah resurrects the people? Rather, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, reprieved him until the day our Qaim (قائمنا) — the one who rises from the progeny of the Prophet to establish justice — is raised; then he will seize him by his forelock and strike his neck. That day is the 'known time'."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, with its chain to Muhammad ibn Yunus, from a man, from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, Blessed and Exalted: فَأَنظِرْنِي until His saying إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ الْوَقْتِ الْمَعْلُومِ. He said: يوم الوقت المعلوم يذبحه رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله ـ على الصخرة التي في بيت المقدس — "On the day of the known time, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) will slay him on the rock that is in Jerusalem."

I say: This is among the reports of the Return (الرجعة) — the belief that certain individuals will return to life before the Day of Resurrection. Regarding its meaning and the meaning of the narration preceding it, there are other reports from the paths of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them).

It is possible that the first of these last three narrations was issued out of dissimulation (التقية), and it is possible to reconcile the three narrations without contradiction based on what preceded in the discussion on the Return in the first volume of the book and elsewhere—that the narrations from the paths of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) in interpreting most verses of the Resurrection interpret them sometimes as the appearance of the Mahdi (peace be upon him), sometimes as the Return, and sometimes as the Resurrection. This is because these three days share in the manifestation of realities, even if they differ in terms of intensity and weakness; thus, the ruling of one of them applies to the others. So understand that.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Jabir, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: I said: "What do you say about the saying of Allah: إِنَّ عِبَادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَانٌ? What is the interpretation of this?" He said: قال الله : إنك لا تملك أن تدخلهم جنة ولا نارا — "Allah said: 'You do not possess the power to enter them into Paradise or the Fire'."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: لَهَا سَبْعَةُ أَبْوَابٍ. He said: يدخل في كل باب أهل مذهب ، وللجنة ثمانية أبواب — "The people of a [particular] school of thought (مذهب) enter through each gate, and Paradise has eight gates."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad recorded in al-Zuhd from Khattab ibn 'Abdillah, who said: 'Ali said: "Do you know how the gates of Hell are?" We said: "Like these gates." He said: لا ، ولكنها هكذا ووضع يده فوق يده وبسط يده على يده — "No, but they are like this," and he placed his hand over his hand and spread his hand over his hand.

In the same source, Ibn al-Mubarak, Hannad, Ibn Abi Shaybah, 'Abd ibn Humayd, Ahmad in al-Zuhd, Ibn Abi al-Dunya in Sifat al-Nar, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Ba'th recorded from various paths from 'Ali, who said: أبواب جهنم سبعة بعضها فوق بعض ـ فيملأ الأول ثم الثاني ثم الثالث حتى يملأ كلها — "The gates of Hell are seven, one above the other; the first is filled, then the second, then the third, until all of them are filled."

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh and al-Khatib in his Tarikh recorded from Anas, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said regarding His saying, the Exalted: لِّكُلِّ بَابٍ مِّنْهُمْ جُزْءٌ مَّقْسُومٌ. He said: جزء أشركوا بالله ، وجزء شكوا في الله ، وجزء غفلوا عن الله — "A portion who associated partners with Allah, a portion who doubted Allah, and a portion who were heedless of Allah."

I say: This is an enumeration of the portions of the gates rather than the gates themselves, and it appears the speech is not intended for restriction (الحصر).

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh recorded from Abu Dharr, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: لجهنم باب لا يدخل منه إلا من أخفرني في أهل بيتي ـ وأراق دماءهم من بعدي — "Hell has a gate through which none shall enter except one who betrayed me regarding my Ahl al-Bayt and shed their blood after me."

I say: It is said: khafarahu, meaning he betrayed him and broke his covenant.

In the same source, Ahmad, Ibn Hibban, al-Tabari, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Ba'th recorded from 'Utbah ibn 'Abdillah, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who said: للجنة ثمانية أبواب وللنار سبعة أبواب ـ وبعضها أفضل من بعض — "Paradise has eight gates and the Fire has seven gates, and some of them are superior to others."

I say: The narrations—as you see—support what we presented.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَنَزَعْنَا مَا فِي صُدُورِهِم مِّنْ غِلٍّ. He said: العداوة — "Enmity."

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from al-Hafiz Abu Nu'aym, from his men, from Abu Hurayrah, who said: 'Ali ibn Abi Talib said: "O Messenger of Allah, which of us is more beloved to you, I or Fatimah?" He said: فاطمة أحب إلي منك وأنت أعز علي منها ، وكأني بك وأنت على حوضي تذود عنه الناس ، وإن عليه أباريق عدد نجوم السماء ، وأنت والحسن والحسين وحمزة وجعفر في الجنة ـ إخوانا على سرر متقابلين وأنت معي وشيعتك — "Fatimah is more beloved to me than you, and you are dearer to me than her. It is as if I see you at my Pond (حوضي) driving people away from it, and upon it are jugs as numerous as the stars of the sky. You, al-Hasan, al-Husayn, Hamzah, and Ja'far are in Paradise—'brothers, on couches, facing one another'—and you are with me and your followers (شيعتك)." Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) recited: إِخْوَانًا عَلَىٰ سُرُرٍ مُّتَقَابِلِينَ — "None of you shall look at the back of his companion's neck."

In the same source, from Ibn al-Maghazili in al-Manaqib, raising it to Zayd ibn Arqam, who said: I entered upon the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and he said: إني مواخ بينكم كما آخى الله بين الملائكة — "I am establishing brotherhood among you just as Allah established brotherhood among the angels." Then he said to 'Ali: "You are my brother." Then he recited this verse: إِخْوَانًا عَلَىٰ سُرُرٍ مُّتَقَابِلِينَ — "The friends in Allah look at one another."

I say: It was also narrated by Ahmad in his Musnad, raised to Zayd ibn Awfa from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and the narration is extensive.

In the two narrations, there is an interpretation of His saying, the Exalted: "on couches, facing one another" by his saying: "None of them looks at the back of his companion's neck," and his saying: "The friends in Allah look at one another." In this is an indication that "facing one another" in the verse is a metaphor for none of them seeking out the faults and slips of his brothers, as is done by one who has rancour in his breast; and this is a subtle meaning.

What the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) recited of the verse is only by way of application (الجري والانطباق) — the principle that Quranic verses apply to new instances beyond their original context — and not that the verse was revealed specifically regarding the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), for the context of the verses does not suit that at all.

Similar to it is what was narrated from 'Ali (peace be upon him): "The verse was revealed regarding us, the people of Badr." In another narration from him (peace be upon him): "It was revealed regarding Abu Bakr and 'Umar." In another narration from 'Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him): "It was revealed regarding Abu Bakr, 'Umar, and 'Ali." In another narration: "It was revealed regarding 'Ali, al-Zubayr, and Talhah." In another narration: "It was revealed regarding 'Ali, 'Uthman, Talhah, and al-Zubayr." In another narration from Ibn 'Abbas: "It was revealed regarding ten: Abu Bakr, 'Umar, 'Uthman, 'Ali, Talhah, al-Zubayr, Sa'd, Sa'id, 'Abd al-Rahman ibn 'Awf, and 'Abdullah ibn Mas'ud."

These narrations—despite their differences—are applications by the narrators. The verse, by its context, refuses to have been revealed regarding some of those mentioned. How so? When it is among a set of verses narrating what Allah decreed and judged on the day He created Adam and commanded the angels and Iblis to prostrate to him, and Iblis refused and He cast him out, then He decreed what He decreed. This has no connection to specific individuals in their particularity.

Ayahs

۞ نَبِّئْ عِبَادِىٓ أَنِّىٓ أَنَا ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٤٩)
Inform my servants that I am indeed the All-forgiving, the All-merciful, (49)
وَأَنَّ عَذَابِى هُوَ ٱلْعَذَابُ ٱلْأَلِيمُ (٥٠)
and that My punishment is a painful punishment. (50)
وَنَبِّئْهُمْ عَن ضَيْفِ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ (٥١)
And inform them about the guests of Abraham, (51)
إِذْ دَخَلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِ فَقَالُوا۟ سَلَـٰمًا قَالَ إِنَّا مِنكُمْ وَجِلُونَ (٥٢)
when they entered into his presence and said, ‘Peace!’ He said, ‘We are indeed afraid of you.’ (52)
قَالُوا۟ لَا تَوْجَلْ إِنَّا نُبَشِّرُكَ بِغُلَـٰمٍ عَلِيمٍ (٥٣)
They said, ‘Do not be afraid. Indeed we give you the good news of a wise son.’ (53)
قَالَ أَبَشَّرْتُمُونِى عَلَىٰٓ أَن مَّسَّنِىَ ٱلْكِبَرُ فَبِمَ تُبَشِّرُونَ (٥٤)
He said, ‘Do you give me good news though old age has befallen me? What is the good news that you bring me?’ (54)
قَالُوا۟ بَشَّرْنَـٰكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ فَلَا تَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْقَـٰنِطِينَ (٥٥)
They said, ‘We bring you good news in truth; so do not be despondent.’ (55)
قَالَ وَمَن يَقْنَطُ مِن رَّحْمَةِ رَبِّهِۦٓ إِلَّا ٱلضَّآلُّونَ (٥٦)
He said, ‘Who despairs of his Lord’s mercy except the astray?!’ (56)
قَالَ فَمَا خَطْبُكُمْ أَيُّهَا ٱلْمُرْسَلُونَ (٥٧)
He said, ‘O messengers, what is now your errand?’ (57)
قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّآ أُرْسِلْنَآ إِلَىٰ قَوْمٍ مُّجْرِمِينَ (٥٨)
They said, ‘We have been sent toward a guilty people, (58)
إِلَّآ ءَالَ لُوطٍ إِنَّا لَمُنَجُّوهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٥٩)
[who shall perish] except the family of Lot. We will indeed deliver all of them, (59)
إِلَّا ٱمْرَأَتَهُۥ قَدَّرْنَآ ۙ إِنَّهَا لَمِنَ ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ (٦٠)
except his wife, [who], We have ordained, will indeed be among those who remain behind.’ (60)
فَلَمَّا جَآءَ ءَالَ لُوطٍ ٱلْمُرْسَلُونَ (٦١)
So when the messengers came to Lot’s family, (61)
قَالَ إِنَّكُمْ قَوْمٌ مُّنكَرُونَ (٦٢)
he said, ‘You are indeed strangers [to me].’ (62)
قَالُوا۟ بَلْ جِئْنَـٰكَ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ فِيهِ يَمْتَرُونَ (٦٣)
They said, ‘Indeed, we bring you what they used to doubt. (63)
وَأَتَيْنَـٰكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَإِنَّا لَصَـٰدِقُونَ (٦٤)
We bring you the truth, and indeed, we speak truly. (64)
فَأَسْرِ بِأَهْلِكَ بِقِطْعٍ مِّنَ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱتَّبِعْ أَدْبَـٰرَهُمْ وَلَا يَلْتَفِتْ مِنكُمْ أَحَدٌ وَٱمْضُوا۟ حَيْثُ تُؤْمَرُونَ (٦٥)
Take your family in a watch of the night; and follow in their rear, and none of you should turn round, and proceed as you are bidden.’ (65)
وَقَضَيْنَآ إِلَيْهِ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْأَمْرَ أَنَّ دَابِرَ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ مَقْطُوعٌ مُّصْبِحِينَ (٦٦)
We apprised him of the matter that these will be rooted out by dawn. (66)
وَجَآءَ أَهْلُ ٱلْمَدِينَةِ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ (٦٧)
The people of the city came, rejoicing. (67)
قَالَ إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ضَيْفِى فَلَا تَفْضَحُونِ (٦٨)
He said, ‘These are indeed my guests. Do not bring dishonour on me. (68)
وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَلَا تُخْزُونِ (٦٩)
Be wary of Allah and do not humiliate me.’ (69)
قَالُوٓا۟ أَوَلَمْ نَنْهَكَ عَنِ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٧٠)
They said, ‘Did we not forbid you from [defending ] strangers?’ (70)
قَالَ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ بَنَاتِىٓ إِن كُنتُمْ فَـٰعِلِينَ (٧١)
He said, ‘These are my daughters, [marry them] if you should do anything.’ (71)
لَعَمْرُكَ إِنَّهُمْ لَفِى سَكْرَتِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ (٧٢)
By your life, they were bewildered in their drunkenness. (72)
فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلصَّيْحَةُ مُشْرِقِينَ (٧٣)
So the Cry seized them at sunrise, (73)
فَجَعَلْنَا عَـٰلِيَهَا سَافِلَهَا وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ حِجَارَةً مِّن سِجِّيلٍ (٧٤)
and We made its topmost part its nethermost, and rained on them stones of shale. (74)
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّلْمُتَوَسِّمِينَ (٧٥)
There are indeed signs in that for the percipient. (75)
وَإِنَّهَا لَبِسَبِيلٍ مُّقِيمٍ (٧٦)
Indeed it is on a standing road, (76)
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٧٧)
and there is indeed a sign in that for the faithful. (77)
وَإِن كَانَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْأَيْكَةِ لَظَـٰلِمِينَ (٧٨)
Indeed the inhabitants of Aykah were [also] wrongdoers. (78)
فَٱنتَقَمْنَا مِنْهُمْ وَإِنَّهُمَا لَبِإِمَامٍ مُّبِينٍ (٧٩)
So We took vengeance on them, and indeed the two of them are on an open highway. (79)
وَلَقَدْ كَذَّبَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْحِجْرِ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (٨٠)
Certainly the inhabitants of Hijr denied the apostles. (80)
وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا فَكَانُوا۟ عَنْهَا مُعْرِضِينَ (٨١)
We had given them Our signs but they disregarded them. (81)
وَكَانُوا۟ يَنْحِتُونَ مِنَ ٱلْجِبَالِ بُيُوتًا ءَامِنِينَ (٨٢)
They used to hew out dwellings from mountains feeling secure. (82)
فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلصَّيْحَةُ مُصْبِحِينَ (٨٣)
So the Cry seized them at dawn, (83)
فَمَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (٨٤)
and what they used to earn did not avail them. (84)

(Commentary)

After the Exalted spoke regarding their mockery of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the Book revealed to him, and their suggestion that he should bring them the angels while they were not believers—even if He were to grant them the clearest of signs—He, the Exalted, presents in these verses a comprehensive statement of glad tidings (التبشير) and warning (الإنذار). This is contained in His saying: نَبِّئْ عِبادِي (Inform My servants) until the end of the two verses. He then clarifies and supports this with a story that encompasses both aspects and includes both matters together: the story of the guests of Ibrahim. In it is the glad tidings to Ibrahim of that which is normally not expected, and the punishment of the people of Lut with the severest types of torment.

Then He, the Exalted, reinforces this with a brief reference to the punishment of the Dwellers of the Ayykah (أصحاب الأيكة)—who were the people of Shu‘ayb—and the Dwellers of al-Hijr (أصحاب الحجر)—who were Thamud, the people of Salih (عليه‌السلام).

His saying, the Exalted: نَبِّئْ عِبادِي أَنِّي أَنَا الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ وَأَنَّ عَذابِي هُوَ الْعَذابُ الْأَلِيمُ — Inform My servants that I am indeed the All-forgiving, the All-merciful, (49) and that My punishment is the painful punishment. (50) What is meant by His saying: عِبادِي (My servants), according to what the context of the verses suggests, is all servants in general. No weight is given to what some have mentioned: that it refers specifically to the God-conscious (المتقون) mentioned previously, or the devoted (المخلصون).

The emphasis of the two sentences through the nominal structure (الجملة الاسمية), the particle إِنَّ (indeed), the separating pronoun (ضمير الفصل), and the definite article الـ in the predicate (الخبر) indicates that the attributes mentioned therein—namely forgiveness (المغفرة), mercy (الرحمة), and the painfulness of the punishment—reach the ultimate limit in their meaning, such that they cannot be measured by any measure, nor can anything else be compared to them. For there is no forgiveness or mercy except that one can imagine an obstacle preventing its bestowal or a measure that limits and defines it. However, He, the Exalted, decrees, and there is none to reverse His decree (لا معقب لحكمه), nor any obstacle that can resist Him. Nothing prevents the fulfillment of His forgiveness and mercy, and nothing limits them unless He, the Majestic and Exalted, wills it. Thus, no one should despair of His forgiveness or lose hope in His relief and mercy based on some preventing obstacle or deterring factor, unless he fears the Exalted Himself, as He said: لا تَقْنَطُوا مِنْ رَحْمَةِ اللهِ إِنَّ اللهَ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعاً إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ وَأَنِيبُوا إِلى رَبِّكُمْ — ‘...do not despair of the mercy of Allah. Indeed Allah forgives all sins. Indeed He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful. (53) And turn to your Lord...’ (Az-Zumar: 53-54).

Similarly, no one should belittle His punishment, or hope for His inability [to punish], or feel secure from His plotting (مكره), for Allah is master over His affair, and none feels secure from the plot of Allah except the people who are losers.

His saying, the Exalted: وَنَبِّئْهُمْ عَنْ ضَيْفِ إِبْراهِيمَ — And inform them about Ibrahim’s guests. (51) The guest (الضيف) is well-known; the term is used for both singular and plural. It is sometimes pluralized as أضياف, ضيوف, and ضيفان, but the most eloquent usage—as has been said—is that it is not dualized or pluralized because it was originally a verbal noun (مصدر).

What is meant by the "guests" are the honored angels who were sent to give Ibrahim glad tidings of a son and to destroy the people of Lut. He called them "guests" because they entered upon him in the form of guests.

His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ دَخَلُوا عَلَيْهِ فَقالُوا سَلاماً قالَ إِنَّا مِنْكُمْ وَجِلُونَ قالُوا لا تَوْجَلْ إِنَّا نُبَشِّرُكَ بِغُلامٍ عَلِيمٍ — when they entered upon him and said, ‘Peace!’ He said, ‘We are indeed afraid of you.’ (52) They said, ‘Do not be afraid. Indeed we give you the good news of a wise son.’ (53) The plural pronoun in دَخَلُوا (they entered) and فَقالُوا (they said) in both instances refers to the angels. Their saying: سَلاماً (Peace) is a greeting, and its implied meaning is "We greet you with peace." Ibrahim’s (عليه‌السلام) saying: إِنَّا مِنْكُمْ وَجِلُونَ means "we are afraid of you," and الوجل is fear (الخوف).

Ibrahim only said this to them after they had settled in the gathering and he had presented to them a roasted calf, but they did not eat from it. When he saw their hands not reaching for it, he became suspicious of them and conceived a fear of them, as mentioned in Surah Hud; thus, the story is mentioned here in a summarized manner.

Their saying: لا تَوْجَلْ (Do not be afraid) was to calm his fear, grant him security, and put his mind at ease by informing him that they were messengers of his Lord and had entered upon him to give him glad tidings of a "wise son" (غلام عليم)—that is, a child who would be a youth and knowledgeable. Perhaps what is meant by his being "wise" (عليم) is his being knowledgeable through the teaching of Allah and His revelation (الوحي), which brings it close to His saying in another place: وَبَشَّرْناهُ بِإِسْحاقَ نَبِيًّا — And We gave him the good news of Isaac, a prophet... (As-Saffat: 112).

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ أَبَشَّرْتُمُونِي عَلى أَنْ مَسَّنِيَ الْكِبَرُ فَبِمَ تُبَشِّرُونَ — He said, ‘Do you give me good news though old age has come upon me? So what is the good news that you give?’ (54) Ibrahim (عليه‌السلام) received the glad tidings while he was a very old man (شيخ كبير هرم) with no offspring from his wife, and the customary course of events (العادة الجارية) had made him despair of having a child, even though he was too exalted to despair of the mercy of Allah and the efficacy of His power. Therefore, he marveled at their statement and questioned them: how could they give him glad tidings of a child while his condition was such? And his wife was a barren old woman, as occurs in another place in the speech of the Exalted.

So his saying: أَبَشَّرْتُمُونِي عَلى أَنْ مَسَّنِيَ الْكِبَرُ — "old age" (الكبر) is a metaphor for senility, and its "touching" him is its reaching him through the exhaustion of his youth and the fading of his strengths. The meaning is: "I marvel at your glad tidings to me while the situation is that I am a decrepit old man; my youth has perished and the strengths of my body are lost, and custom dictates that one in such a state does not have a child born to him."

His saying: فَبِمَ تُبَشِّرُونَ (So what is the good news that you give?) is a derivation from his saying: مَسَّنِيَ الْكِبَرُ. It is an inquiry about what they were giving him glad tidings of, as if he were doubting whether their glad tidings were indeed about a child—despite their explicit statement—not because he deemed it impossible, but rather asking: "What is this that you bring news of? For that which the literal sense of your speech indicates is a marvelous thing." This is common in speech; a man says when informed of something he deems unlikely or does not believe: "What are you saying?" or "What do you mean?" or "What are you doing?"

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا بَشَّرْناكَ بِالْحَقِ — until His saying — إِلَّا الضَّالُّونَ — They said, ‘We have given you good news in truth; so do not be among the despondent.’ (55) He said, ‘And who despairs of his Lord’s mercy except the astray?’ (56) The preposition بـ in بِالْحَقِ (in truth) denotes accompaniment; that is, "our glad tidings are inseparable from the truth and not detached from it, so do not reject it through deeming it unlikely, lest you be among those who despair of the mercy of Allah." This was the reply of the angels. Ibrahim (عليه‌السلام) responded to them by way of allusion (التكنية), saying: وَمَنْ يَقْنَطُ مِنْ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّهِ إِلَّا الضَّالُّونَ (And who despairs of his Lord’s mercy except the astray?). The question is rhetorical/disavowing (إنكاري); meaning that despairing of the mercy of Allah is something restricted to the astray (الضالون), and I am not astray, so my question is not the question of one who despairs or deems it impossible.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ فَما خَطْبُكُمْ أَيُّهَا الْمُرْسَلُونَ — He said, ‘O messengers, what is your errand now?’ (57) The "errand" (الخطب) is a momentous matter and a great affair. In addressing them as "messengers" (المرسلون), there is an indication that they had mentioned this to him previously. The meaning of the verse is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا إِنَّا أُرْسِلْنا إِلى قَوْمٍ مُجْرِمِينَ — until His saying — لَمِنَ الْغابِرِينَ — They said, ‘We have been sent to a guilty people, (58) except the family of Lot; we will indeed save them all, (59) except his wife: we have ordained that she shall indeed be among those who remain behind.’ (60) It is said in al-Mufradat: "The one who remains behind (الغابر) is the one who stays after those who were with him have passed." The Exalted said: إِلَّا عَجُوزاً فِي الْغابِرِينَ (except an old woman among those who remained behind), meaning among those whose lives were prolonged. It is also said: among those who stayed and did not travel with Lut. And it is said: among those who remained behind in the punishment. In another verse: إِلَّا امْرَأَتَكَ كانَتْ مِنَ الْغابِرِينَ (except your wife; she was of those who remained behind). And in another: قَدَّرْنا إِنَّها لَمِنَ الْغابِرِينَ (We have ordained that she shall indeed be among those who remain behind). He continues: "Dust (الغبار) is what remains of stirred-up earth, and it is formed on the pattern of smoke (الدخان) and stumbling (العثار) and similar terms for remnants." [End of quote]. Perhaps it is from here that both the past and the future are sometimes called غابر; as for the past, it is with the consideration that it remained in what has passed and did not cross over into the present time; as for the future, it is with the consideration that it is remaining and has not yet perished like the past.

The verses are the angels' answer to Ibrahim's question: قالُوا إِنَّا أُرْسِلْنا (They said, ‘We have been sent’) from Allah, the Glorified, إِلى قَوْمٍ مُجْرِمِينَ (to a guilty people). They referred to them indefinitely and did not name them to protect the tongue from explicitly mentioning their name out of aversion to it, and the subsequent speech identifies them. Then they made an exception and said: إِلَّا آلَ لُوطٍ (except the family of Lut), who were Lut and his close ones, and through this, it became clear that the "people" were his people. إِنَّا لَمُنَجُّوهُمْ (we will indeed save them)—that is, deliver them from the punishment—أَجْمَعِينَ (all). The apparent context suggests that the exception is disconnected (منقطع).

Then they excepted Lut’s wife from his family to indicate that the salvation would not include her and that the punishment would seize and destroy her. So they said: إِلَّا امْرَأَتَهُ قَدَّرْنا إِنَّها لَمِنَ الْغابِرِينَ (except his wife: we have ordained that she shall indeed be among those who remain behind)—meaning those who stayed behind from the people after the family of Lut departed from their town.

The detailed discussion regarding the guests of Ibrahim (عليه‌السلام) has already been presented in Surah Hud in the tenth volume of this book, where we dedicated an independent study to it.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا جاءَ آلَ لُوطٍ الْمُرْسَلُونَ قالَ إِنَّكُمْ قَوْمٌ مُنْكَرُونَ — So when the messengers came to the family of Lot, (61) he said, ‘You are indeed a people unknown [to me].’ (62) Lut (عليه‌السلام) only said that to them because they appeared in the form of handsome, beardless youths, and he was distressed by what he saw of them, given the state of his people regarding indecency (الفحشاء), as previously mentioned in Surah Hud. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا بَلْ جِئْناكَ بِما كانُوا فِيهِ يَمْتَرُونَ وَأَتَيْناكَ بِالْحَقِّ وَإِنَّا لَصادِقُونَ — They said, ‘Indeed, we have bring you what they used to doubt. (63) We bring you the truth, and we are indeed truthful.’ (64) Doubting (الامتراء) comes from المرية, which is doubt (الشك). What is meant by "what they used to doubt" is the punishment with which Lut used to warn them while they doubted it. What is meant by "bringing the truth" is their bringing a truthful decree (قضاء حق) regarding the matter of the people from which there is no turning back, as occurred in another place in their saying: وَإِنَّهُمْ آتِيهِمْ عَذابٌ غَيْرُ مَرْدُودٍ — ‘...and indeed there is coming to them a punishment that is irreversible.’ (Hud: 76). It is also said: what is meant is "We bring you the punishment about which there is no doubt," but what we have mentioned is the correct view.

In the verses of the story, there is a transposition (تقديم وتأخير)—not in the sense of a disruption in the order of their revelation during compilation by placing what is later in the position of what is earlier and vice versa, but rather in the sense that He, the Exalted, mentioned some parts of the story in a place other than what the natural order (الترتيب الطبعي) requires and what the method of narration (سنة الاقتصاص) dictates, due to a subtle point (نكتة) that necessitates it.

The order of the story according to its parts—as Allah, the Glorified, mentioned it in Surah Hud and elsewhere, and as reason supports—requires that His saying: فَلَمَّا جاءَ آلَ لُوطٍ (So when [the messengers] came to the family of Lot) until the end of two verses, should be before the other verses. Then His saying: وَجاءَ أَهْلُ الْمَدِينَةِ (And the people of the city came) until the end of six verses. Then His saying: قالُوا بَلْ جِئْناكَ (They said, ‘Indeed, we have bring you...’) until the end of four verses. Then His saying: فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الصَّيْحَةُ مُشْرِقِينَ (So the Cry seized them at sunrise) until the end of the verses.

The reality of this transposition is that the story has four chapters (فصول). The third chapter was taken and placed between the first and the second. That is, His saying: وَجاءَ أَهْلُ الْمَدِينَةِ until the end was delayed in mention so that its end—which is His saying: لَعَمْرُكَ إِنَّهُمْ لَفِي سَكْرَتِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ (By your life, they were indeed bewildered in their intoxication)—would be connected to the beginning of the final chapter: فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الصَّيْحَةُ مُشْرِقِينَ (So the Cry seized them at sunrise). This was done so that the purpose of citing the story as evidence would be fully realized and manifest with the utmost clarity: namely, the descent of a terrifying punishment like theirs while they were in a state of intoxication and a sense of security from it, with nothing of the sort crossing their minds. This is more profound in causing shock and more effective in inducing regret, adding pain upon pain to the punishment.

Similar to this in its allusion to this subtle point is what occurs at the end of the story of the Dwellers of al-Hijr that follows, where His saying: وَكانُوا يَنْحِتُونَ مِنَ الْجِبالِ بُيُوتاً آمِنِينَ (They used to hollow out dwellings from the mountains, [feeling] secure) is connected to His saying: فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الصَّيْحَةُ مُصْبِحِينَ (So the Cry seized them at dawn). All of this is to manifest the meaning of His saying, the Exalted, at the beginning of the discourse: وَأَنَّ عَذابِي هُوَ الْعَذابُ الْأَلِيمُ (and that My punishment is the painful punishment). So understand that.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَسْرِ بِأَهْلِكَ بِقِطْعٍ مِنَ اللَّيْلِ — until the end of the verse — ‘So set out with your family in a watch of the night, and follow in their rear, and let none of you look back, and proceed as you are bidden.’ (65) Setting out by night (الإسراء) is traveling during the night, so His saying: بِقِطْعٍ مِنَ اللَّيْلِ (in a watch of the night) emphasizes it. A "watch of the night" (قطع الليل) is a portion cut from it. What is meant by "following in their rear" (اتباعه أدبارهم) is that he should walk behind them so as not to leave anyone to fall behind the march, and to urge them to travel swiftly, as suggested by His saying: وَلا يَلْتَفِتْ مِنْكُمْ أَحَدٌ (and let none of you look back).

The meaning is: "Since We have brought you an irreversible punishment and a decree from Allah that is final, you must travel with your family by night and you yourself take the rear so that they do not fall behind or become lax in the journey; let none of you look back, and proceed to where you are commanded." In this is an indication that there was divine guidance (هداية إلهية) before them guiding them and a leader leading them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَضَيْنا إِلَيْهِ ذلِكا الْأَمْرَ أَنَّ دابِرَ هؤُلاءِ مَقْطُوعٌ مُصْبِحِينَ — And We apprised him of the matter: that the last remnant of these shall be cut off by dawn. (66) The "decreeing" (القضاء) here encompasses the meaning of revelation (الوحي), which is why it is made transitive with the preposition إلى (to)—as has been said. What is meant by "the matter" (الأمر) is the matter of the punishment, as explained by His saying: أَنَّ دابِرَ هؤُلاءِ مَقْطُوعٌ مُصْبِحِينَ (that the last remnant of these shall be cut off by dawn). The reference to it with the word ذلِكَ (that) is to indicate the greatness of its gravity and the horror of its affair.

The meaning is: "We decreed Our great matter regarding their punishment, revealing that to Lut: that the remnant (دابر) of these people and their trace which would normally remain after them—of progeny, buildings, and deeds—is to be cut off while they are entering the morning." Or the implied structure is: "We revealed to him, decreeing, etc."

His saying, the Exalted: وَجاءَ أَهْلُ الْمَدِينَةِ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ — until His saying — إِنْ كُنْتُمْ فاعِلِينَ — And the people of the city came rejoicing. (67) He said, ‘These are indeed my guests. So do not disgrace me. (68) And be wary of Allah and do not humiliate me.’ (69) They said, ‘Did we not forbid you from [protecting] all people?’ (70) He said, ‘These are my daughters, if you should do [anything].’ (71) Attributing the "coming" to the "people of the city" indicates that they were a large group that could correctly be called the people of the city due to their numbers.

So the meaning is: "And there came" to Lut "the people of the city"—a large gathering of them—wanting his guests, while they were "rejoicing" (يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ) because of their obsession with indecency, especially with those entering their lands from outside. Lut met them, defending his guests: قالَ إِنَّ هؤُلاءِ ضَيْفِي فَلا تَفْضَحُونِ (He said, ‘These are indeed my guests. So do not disgrace me’) by committing the foul act with them. وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ وَلا تُخْزُونِ (And be wary of Allah and do not humiliate me). The attackers from the people of the city said: "Did we not cut off your excuse in sheltering them? أَوَلَمْ نَنْهَكَ عَنِ الْعالَمِينَ (Did we not forbid you from [protecting] all people?)—that you should shelter them, intercede for them, or defend them?" When Lut (عليه‌السلام) despaired of them, he offered them his daughters so they would turn away from his guests by marrying them—as previously explained in Surah Hud—قالَ هؤُلاءِ بَناتِي إِنْ كُنْتُمْ فاعِلِينَ (He said, ‘These are my daughters, if you should do [anything]’).

His saying, the Exalted: لَعَمْرُكَ إِنَّهُمْ لَفِي سَكْرَتِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ — until His saying — مِنْ سِجِّيلٍ — By your life, they were indeed bewildered in their intoxication. (72) So the Cry seized them at sunrise. (73) And We made its high part its low part, and We rained upon them stones of shale. (74) It is said in al-Mufradat: "Cultivation (العمارة) is the opposite of ruin (الخراب)." He said: "العمر (life/age) is a name for the duration of the body's cultivation by life; it is less than 'subsistence' (البقاء). When it is said: 'his life (عمره) was long,' it means the cultivation of his body by his soul. When 'his subsistence' (بقاؤه) is said, it does not necessarily imply that, for subsistence is the opposite of annihilation (الفناء). Due to the superiority of subsistence over life, Allah described Himself with it, and He is rarely described with العمر." He said: "العمر (with damma) and العمر (with fatha) are the same, but the oath is specifically with العمر (with fatha) and not العمر (with damma), such as لَعَمْرُكَ إِنَّهُمْ لَفِي سَكْرَتِهِمْ." [End of quote].

The address in لَعَمْرُكَ (By your life) is to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله); thus, it is an oath by his continued existence. The statement of some that it is an address from the angels to Lut (عليه‌السلام) and an oath by his life has no evidence from the context of the verses.

Bewilderment (العمه) is to wander in confusion. Shale (السجيل) refers to the stones of punishment. The detailed discussion of its meaning has already been presented in the commentary on Surah Hud.

The meaning is: "I swear by your life and your continued existence, O Muhammad, they were indeed in their intoxication—which is their heedlessness due to being submerged in indecency and evil—wandering in confusion. فأخذتهم الصيحة (So the Cry seized them)—which is the terrifying sound—مشرقين (at sunrise)—that is, while they were entering the radiance of the morning. So We made the high part of their lands its low part, and its top its bottom, and We rained and sent down from the sky upon them stones of shale."

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِلْمُتَوَسِّمِينَ — until His saying — لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ — There are indeed signs in that for the percipient. (75) Indeed they are on a standing road. (76) There is indeed a sign in that for the faithful. (77) The sign (الآية) is the mark (العلامة). What is meant by "signs" (آيات) first are the marks indicating the occurrence of the event from the remaining ruins. What is meant by "a sign" (آية) second is the mark that indicates to the believers the truth of the warning and the divine call. Percipience (التوسم) is intuition (التفرس) and the transition from the outward features (سيماء) of things to the reality of their state.

The meaning is: "In that—that is, in what occurred of the matter to the people of Lut and in their lands—are marks from the remaining ruins for the intuitive (المتفرسين). And those marks are on a path for travelers that is standing (مقيم), not having been completely effaced or erased yet. Indeed, in that is a sign for the believers indicating the truth of the warning and the call." Through this, the reason for mentioning "signs" in the plural and "sign" in the singular in the two places becomes clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ كانَ أَصْحابُ الْأَيْكَةِ لَظالِمِينَ — until — فَانْتَقَمْنا مِنْهُمْ وَإِنَّهُما لَبِإِمامٍ مُبِينٍ — The Dwellers of the Ayykah were indeed wrongdoers. (78) So We took vengeance on them, and indeed the two of them are on a manifest highway. (79) The ayykah (الأيكة) is a single instance of ayyk, which refers to trees intertwined with one another. They were—as has been said—in a thicket (غيضة), which is a spot dense with trees.

These—as has been mentioned—were the people of Shu‘ayb (عليه‌السلام) or a group of his people who lived in the thicket. This is supported by His saying at the end: وَإِنَّهُما لَبِإِمامٍ مُبِينٍ (and indeed the two of them are on a manifest highway)—meaning the locations of the people of Lut and the Dwellers of the Ayykah are on a clear road. For that which is on the road from Medina to Syria are the ruined lands of the people of Lut and the people of Shu‘ayb. Allah destroyed them because of their disbelief and their denial of the call of Shu‘ayb (عليه‌السلام). Their story has already been presented in Surah Hud. His saying: فَانْتَقَمْنا مِنْهُمْ (So We took vengeance on them)—the pronoun refers to the Dwellers of the Ayykah, and it is said: to them and the people of Lut. The meaning of the two verses is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ كَذَّبَ أَصْحابُ الْحِجْرِ الْمُرْسَلِينَ — until His saying — ما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ — Certainly the inhabitants of al-Hijr denied the messengers. (80) We gave them Our signs, but they were oblivious to them. (81) They used to hollow out dwellings from the mountains, [feeling] secure. (82) So the Cry seized them at dawn, (83) and what they used to earn did not avail them. (84) The Dwellers of al-Hijr (أصحاب الحجر) were Thamud, the people of Salih. Al-Hijr is the name of a town they inhabited. He counted them as deniers of all the messengers—even though they only denied Salih, the messenger sent to them—because the call of the messengers is a single call, and the one who denies one of them has denied them all.

His saying: وَآتَيْناهُمْ آياتِنا فَكانُوا عَنْها مُعْرِضِينَ (We gave them Our signs, but they were oblivious to them)—if what is meant by "signs" are miracles (المعجزات) and extraordinary events (الخوارق)—as is apparent—then what is meant by them is the She-camel, her drinking, and what appeared to them after she was hamstrung until they were destroyed. The story has already been presented in Surah Hud. If what is meant by them are the divine gnoses (المعارف الإلهية) that Salih (عليه‌السلام) conveyed and spread among them, or the combination of true gnoses and the miraculous sign, then the matter is clear.

His saying: وَكانُوا يَنْحِتُونَ مِنَ الْجِبالِ بُيُوتاً آمِنِينَ (They used to hollow out dwellings from the mountains, [feeling] secure)—meaning they used to live in caves and caverns carved from stone, feeling secure from earthly and heavenly accidents, according to their claim.

His saying: فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الصَّيْحَةُ مُصْبِحِينَ (So the Cry seized them at dawn)—meaning the cry of punishment in which was their destruction. Reference has already been made to the suitability of combining "security" with the "Cry" in the two verses with His saying at the beginning of the verses: وَأَنَّ عَذابِي هُوَ الْعَذابُ الْأَلِيمُ (and that My punishment is the painful punishment).

His saying: فَما أَغْنى عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ (and what they used to earn did not avail them)—meaning of the deeds to secure their happiness in life.

(Narrative Discussion)

In ad-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn al-Mundhir and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Mus‘ab ibn Thabit who said: "The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) passed by some of his companions who were laughing. He said: 'Remember Paradise and remember the Fire.' Then there was revealed: نَبِّئْ عِبادِي أَنِّي أَنَا الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ (Inform My servants that I am indeed the All-forgiving, the All-merciful)."

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning, but there is some obscurity in the correspondence of the verse's meaning to the cause mentioned therein.

In the same source, Abu Nu‘aym narrated in al-Hilyah from Ja‘far ibn Muhammad regarding His saying: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِلْمُتَوَسِّمِينَ (There are indeed signs in that for the percipient). He said: "They are the intuitive (المتفرسون)."

In the same source, al-Bukhari narrated in his Tarikh, and at-Tirmidhi, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn as-Sunni, and Abu Nu‘aym together in at-Tibb, and Ibn Mardawayh and al-Khatib from Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Beware of the intuition (فراسة) of the believer, for he looks with the light of Allah.' Then he recited: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِلْمُتَوَسِّمِينَ (There are indeed signs in that for the percipient). He said: 'The intuitive (المتفرسين).'"

In al-Ikhtisas of al-Mufid, with his chain of narration from Abu Bakr ibn Muhammad al-Hadrami from Abu Ja‘far (عليه‌السلام), who said: "He said: 'There is no creature except that between his eyes is written "believer" or "disbeliever"—and that is veiled from you but not veiled from the Imams from the Family of Muhammad—then no one enters upon them except that they recognize him as a believer or a disbeliever.' Then he recited this verse: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِلْمُتَوَسِّمِينَ (There are indeed signs in that for the percipient), for they are the percipient (المتوسمون)."

I say: The narrations on this meaning are numerous and mutually supporting (متظافرة متكاثرة), and their meaning is not that the verse was revealed specifically concerning them (عليه‌السلام).

In ad-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh and Ibn ‘Asakir narrated from Ibn ‘Umar who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Indeed, Madyan and the Dwellers of the Ayykah are two nations to whom Allah sent Shu‘ayb.'"

I say: We have already presented what must be presented of the narrations regarding the story of the glad tidings to Ibrahim and the stories of Lut, Shu‘ayb, and Salih (عليه‌السلام) in the commentary on Surah Hud in the tenth volume of this book, and we have deemed that sufficient instead of repeating them here; so let one refer back there.

Ayahs

وَمَا خَلَقْنَا ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَآ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ لَـَٔاتِيَةٌ ۖ فَٱصْفَحِ ٱلصَّفْحَ ٱلْجَمِيلَ (٨٥)
We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except with consummate wisdom, and indeed the Hour is bound to come. So forbear with a graceful forbearance. (85)
إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ ٱلْخَلَّـٰقُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٨٦)
Indeed your Lord is the All-creator, the All-knowing. (86)
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَـٰكَ سَبْعًا مِّنَ ٱلْمَثَانِى وَٱلْقُرْءَانَ ٱلْعَظِيمَ (٨٧)
Certainly We have given you [the surah of] the seven oft-repeated verses and the great Quran. (87)
لَا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ إِلَىٰ مَا مَتَّعْنَا بِهِۦٓ أَزْوَٰجًا مِّنْهُمْ وَلَا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَٱخْفِضْ جَنَاحَكَ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٨٨)
Do not extend your glance toward what We have provided to certain groups of them, and do not grieve for them, and lower your wing to the faithful, (88)
وَقُلْ إِنِّىٓ أَنَا ٱلنَّذِيرُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (٨٩)
and say, ‘I am indeed a manifest warner [of punishment from God],’ (89)
كَمَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَى ٱلْمُقْتَسِمِينَ (٩٠)
like that We sent down upon those who split into bands, (90)
ٱلَّذِينَ جَعَلُوا۟ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ عِضِينَ (٩١)
who represented the Quran as magic. (91)
فَوَرَبِّكَ لَنَسْـَٔلَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٩٢)
By your Lord, We will question them all (92)
عَمَّا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٩٣)
concerning what they used to do. (93)
فَٱصْدَعْ بِمَا تُؤْمَرُ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (٩٤)
So proclaim what you have been commanded, and turn away from the polytheists. (94)
إِنَّا كَفَيْنَـٰكَ ٱلْمُسْتَهْزِءِينَ (٩٥)
Indeed We will suffice you against the deriders (95)
ٱلَّذِينَ يَجْعَلُونَ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ ۚ فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ (٩٦)
—those who set up another deity besides Allah. Soon they will know! (96)
وَلَقَدْ نَعْلَمُ أَنَّكَ يَضِيقُ صَدْرُكَ بِمَا يَقُولُونَ (٩٧)
Certainly We know that you become upset because of what they say. (97)
فَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ وَكُن مِّنَ ٱلسَّـٰجِدِينَ (٩٨)
So celebrate the praise of your Lord and be among those who prostrate, (98)
وَٱعْبُدْ رَبَّكَ حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِيَكَ ٱلْيَقِينُ (٩٩)
and worship your Lord until certainty comes to you. (99)

(Commentary)

The verses conclude with the objective of the preceding discourse, which is the command to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to openly proclaim what he is commanded, to adopt forgiveness, and to turn away from the polytheists (المشركين). He is told not to grieve over them nor to let his breast be straitened by what they say. Indeed, it is part of the True Decree (القضاء الحق) that people be recompensed for their deeds in this world and the Hereafter, especially on the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة), about which there is no doubt. It is the day that leaves no one behind and does not omit an atom’s weight of good or evil without attaching it to its doer. Thus, one should not despair over the disbelief of a disbeliever, for Allah is All-Knowing (عليم) of him and will recompense him; nor should one grieve over him, for preoccupation with Allah, the Glorified, is more important and more obligatory.

The Glorified has repeated His command to him to practice forgiveness and to turn away from those mockers—who were mentioned at the opening of the Surah—and to occupy himself with His glorification, His praise, and His worship (عبادته). He informed him that He has sufficed him against their evil, so let him occupy himself with what Allah has commanded him. With this, the Surah concludes.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ ، وَإِنَّ السَّاعَةَ لَآتِيَةٌ — We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except with the truth, and indeed the Hour is bound to come. (15:85). The preposition "with" (بـ) in His saying "with the truth" (بالحق) denotes accompaniment (المصاحبة); meaning that the creation of all of them is inseparable from the truth and is accompanied by it. Thus, creation has a goal (غاية) to which it shall return. The Exalted said: إِنَّ إِلى رَبِّكَ الرُّجْعى — Indeed, the return is to your Lord. (96:8). Were it not for that, it would have been a vain play (لعبا باطلا). The Exalted said: وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما لاعِبِينَ ما خَلَقْناهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ — We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them for play. We did not create them except with the truth. (44:38-39). And He said: وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماءَ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما باطِلاً — We did not create the heaven and the earth and whatever is between them in vain. (38:27). Evidence that "the truth" (الحق) here refers to that which stands in opposition to vain play is the concluding of the statement with His saying: "and indeed the Hour is bound to come" (وَإِنَّ السَّاعَةَ لَآتِيَةٌ), which is evident.

By this, the invalidity of what some have mentioned becomes clear—that "the truth" (الحق) means justice and equity (العدل والإنصاف), and that the preposition (بـ) denotes causality (السببية), meaning: "We did not create that except because of justice and equity on the Day of Recompense for deeds."

This is because there is no linguistic evidence for "the truth" in the verse meaning justice and equity. Furthermore, the meaning they mentioned would only be consistent if the preposition (بـ) meant the "lam" of purpose (لام الغرض) or accompaniment, rather than causality.

Similarly invalid is what some have mentioned—that "the truth" means wisdom (الحكمة), and that the first sentence "We did not create..." etc., refers to worldly punishment, while the second "and indeed the Hour is bound to come" refers to the punishment of the Hereafter. The meaning would then be: "We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except as being clothed in truth and wisdom, such that the persistence of corruption and the establishment of evils are not compatible with it. Wisdom has necessitated the destruction of the likes of these people to repel their corruption and to guide those who remain toward righteousness; and indeed the Hour is bound to come, wherein vengeance will also be taken against the likes of these."

In this verse, there is a dispute between the proponents of compulsion (أصحاب الجبر) and the proponents of delegation (أصحاب التفويض), each group pulling the fire toward its own loaf. The proponents of compulsion used it as evidence that the acts of servants are created by Allah, because their deeds are among "whatever is between them," so they are created by Him.

The proponents of delegation used it as evidence that the acts of servants are not created by Him, but rather by themselves; for sins and ugly deeds are part of falsehood (الباطل), and if they were created by Him, they would be created "with the truth," and falsehood cannot be created with the truth.

The truth is that both arguments are invalid. The aspects of ugliness and sin in actions are non-existential (عدمية) qualities. For obedience and disobedience—like marriage and adultery, or consuming wealth lawfully or through falsehood, and the like—share the same essential act. They only differ as obedience or disobedience based on compliance with the command or violation of it, and violation is a non-existential aspect. Since this is the case, the attribution of the act to creation from the perspective of existence (الوجود) does not necessitate the attribution of the ugly or the sinful to it, for those are among its non-existential aspects. Thus, the act, in its non-existential aspect, is not part of "whatever is between the heavens and the earth" such that the verse would include it; nor is it, in its existential aspect, part of falsehood such that its creation would be the creation of falsehood with the truth.

Furthermore, necessity dictates the governance of the system of causes and effects (نظام العلل والمعلولات) in existence, and that the subsistence of the existence of a thing through another—such that it cannot be independent without it—is the criterion for attribution (الاتصاف). Thus, the one characterized by obedience, disobedience, the beauty of an act, or its ugliness is the human being, not the One who created him and facilitated for him to do such-and-such. Just as the one characterized by blackness or whiteness is the body in which these two colors subsist, not the One who brought it into existence.

We have fully covered the discussion on this topic in the commentary (تفسير) on His saying: وَما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ — and He does not mislead by it any except the transgressors (2:26) in the first volume of this book.

His saying, the Exalted: فَاصْفَحِ الصَّفْحَ الْجَمِيلَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ الْخَلَّاقُ الْعَلِيمُ — So dispense a graceful pardon. Indeed your Lord is the All-Creator, the All-Knowing. (15:85-86). It is said in al-Mufradat: "The safh (صفح) of a thing is its breadth and its side, like the safhah (surface) of the face, the safhah of the sword, and the safhah of the stone. Al-Safh (الصفح) is to abandon rebuke (التثريب), and it is more profound than al-afw (العفو) — forgiveness. Therefore, He said: فَاعْفُوا وَاصْفَحُوا حَتَّى يَأْتِيَ اللهُ بِأَمْرِهِ — So pardon and be indulgent until Allah ordains His edict. (2:109). A person may pardon (يعفو) but not dispense a graceful pardon (يصفح). The Exalted said: فَاصْفَحْ عَنْهُمْ وَقُلْ سَلامٌ — So disregard them and say, ‘Peace!’ (43:89); فَاصْفَحِ الصَّفْحَ الْجَمِيلَ — So dispense a graceful pardon (15:85); and أَفَنَضْرِبُ عَنْكُمُ الذِّكْرَ صَفْحاً — Shall We ignore you and withdraw the Reminder? (43:5).

And 'I dispensed a graceful pardon to him' (صفحت عنه) means I turned a beautiful side to him, turning away from his sin; or I met his side while being distant from him; or I bypassed the page (safhah) in which his sin was recorded in the book to another, from the expression 'I leafed through (tasaffah-tu) the book.' His saying: 'Indeed the Hour is bound to come, so dispense a graceful pardon' is a command to him (peace be upon him) to lighten the burden of the disbelief of whoever disbelieves, as He said: وَلا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلا تَكُ فِي ضَيْقٍ مِمَّا يَمْكُرُونَ — Do not grieve for them, and do not be upset by their plotting. (16:127). And al-musafahah (handshaking) is the meeting of the surfaces (safhah) of the hands." [End of quote].

There will follow in the section on narrations (الرواية) the interpretation (تفسير) of Imam Ali (peace be upon him) of al-safh as forgiveness without reproach.

His saying: "So dispense a graceful pardon" (فَاصْفَحِ الصَّفْحَ الْجَمِيلَ) is a derivation from what preceded it. That is: since creation is with the truth, and there is a day in which they will be held to account and recompensed—about which there is no doubt—then do not occupy yourself with the denial and mockery you see from them. Forgive them without falling into reproach, dispute, or argument with them. For your Lord, who created you and created them, is All-Knowing (عليم) of your state and their state, and ahead of them is a day they cannot escape.

From here, it becomes clear that His saying: "Indeed your Lord is the All-Creator, the All-Knowing" (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ الْخَلَّاقُ الْعَلِيمُ) is the justification (تعليل) for His saying: "So dispense a graceful pardon."

These verses surrounding His saying: "So proclaim what you are commanded" (فَاصْدَعْ بِما تُؤْمَرُ) are a consolation (تسلية) for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and a soothing of his soul, so that His saying: "So proclaim what you are commanded" may find its proper place. You have learned at the beginning of the Surah that the primary objective of it is the command to announce the call (الدعوة), and you have also learned by reflecting on the previous verses that they are arranged to lead into consoling him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) for the harm, insult, and mockery he faced from his people, and to transition from that to the required command.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْناكَ سَبْعاً مِنَ الْمَثانِي وَالْقُرْآنَ الْعَظِيمَ — Certainly We have given you the seven of the oft-repeated [verses] and the great Quran. (15:87). The "Seven Oft-Repeated" (السبع المثاني) is Surah al-Hamd, according to what has been interpreted in several narrations transmitted from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them). Therefore, no heed should be paid to what some have mentioned: that they are the seven long Surahs (السبع الطوال); or what others mentioned: that they are the seven Surahs beginning with Ha-Mim (الحواميم السبع); or what has been said: that they are seven scrolls from the scrolls revealed to the prophets. There is no evidence for any of these from the wording of the Book nor from the Sunnah.

There has been much disagreement regarding His saying: "of the oft-repeated" (مِنَ الْمَثانِي) as to whether "of" (مِنَ) denotes partitivity (التبعيض) or clarification (التبيين), and regarding the derivation of the word al-mathani (المثاني) and the reason for it being so named.

What ought to be said—and Allah knows best—is that "of" (مِنَ) denotes partitivity. For the Glorified has named all the verses of His Book mathani (oft-repeated) when He said: كِتاباً مُتَشابِهاً مَثانِيَ تَقْشَعِرُّ مِنْهُ جُلُودُ الَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ — a Book, consistent in its parts, and oft-repeated, whereat quiver the skins of those who fear their Lord (39:23). The verses of Surah al-Hamd are among them; thus, they are some of the mathani, not all of them.

It appears that al-mathani (المثاني) is the plural of mathniyyah (مثنية), a passive participle from al-thany (الثني) meaning twisting, bending, or repeating. The Exalted said: يَثْنُونَ صُدُورَهُمْ — they fold up their breasts (11:5). The Quranic verses were named mathani because some of them clarify the state of others and bend or incline toward them, as suggested by His saying: "a Book, consistent in its parts, and oft-repeated" (كِتاباً مُتَشابِهاً مَثانِيَ), where He combined the Book being consistent (mutashabihan)—meaning its verses resemble one another—and its verses being mathani. In the speech of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) describing the Quran: "Some of it confirms the other" (يصدق بعضه بعضا). And from Imam Ali (peace be upon him) regarding it: "Some of it speaks through the other, and some of it bears witness to the other" (ينطق بعضه ببعض ويشهد بعضه على بعض). Or it is the plural of mathna (مثنى) in the sense of repetition and recurrence, as a metaphor for the clarification of some verses by others.

Perhaps this is sufficient and renders unnecessary the various meanings they have mentioned, such as in al-Kashshaf and its glosses, al-Majma', Ruh al-Ma'ani, and others. For example, their saying: it is from tathniyah (doubling) or thany in the sense of repetition and recurrence; the verses of the Quran were named mathani because the meanings are repeated in them. Or their saying: al-Fatihah was named mathani because of the obligation to recite it twice in every prayer, or because it is repeated in every unit (rak'ah) with what is recited after it of the Quran, or because many of its words are repeated like "the All-Beneficent" (الرحمن), "the Most Merciful" (الرحيم), "You" (إياك), "the path" (الصراط), and "upon them" (عليهم). Or because it was revealed twice, once in Mecca and once in Medina; or because of the praise (ثناء) of Allah it contains; or because Allah excepted it and reserved it for this nation and did not reveal it to past nations, as in the narration; among other views mentioned in the commentaries.

In His saying: "the seven of the oft-repeated and the great Quran" (سَبْعاً مِنَ الْمَثانِي وَالْقُرْآنَ الْعَظِيمَ), there is an exaltation of the matter of al-Fatihah and the Quran that is not hidden. As for the Quran, it is due to its being described from the realm of greatness and majesty as "great" (العظيم). As for al-Fatihah, it is due to its being expressed as an undescribed indefinite noun "seven" (سَبْعاً), which contains an indication of the greatness of its value and the majesty of its status that is not hidden, and it has been placed in parallel with the Great Quran, even though it is a part of it.

The verse—as has become clear—is in the context of conferring a favor (الامتنان). Moreover, because it occurs in the context of the call to forgiveness and turning away, it implies that in this greatest gift, which contains the realities of divine knowledge (المعارف الإلهية) that guide to every perfection and happiness by Allah's permission, there is that which should enable you to dispense a graceful pardon, to occupy yourself with your Lord, and to immerse yourself in His obedience.

His saying, the Exalted: لا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ إِلى ما مَتَّعْنا بِهِ أَزْواجاً مِنْهُمْ — Do not extend your glance toward what We have provided certain groups of them with... until His saying: الْمُبِينُ — the manifest [warner] (15:88-89). These two verses are in the position of clarifying the "graceful pardon" (الصفح الجميل) that was previously commanded. Therefore, the speech was brought in the form of a new sentence (استئناف).

Mentioned in them are four directives: two negative and two positive. His saying: "Do not extend your glance toward what We have provided certain groups of them with" (لا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ إِلى ما مَتَّعْنا بِهِ أَزْواجاً مِنْهُمْ)—extending the eyes toward what they have been provided with of the splendor of the life of this world is a metaphor for transgressing by not limiting one's sight to the blessing Allah has given. What is meant by "groups" (أزواجاً) are pairs of men and women, or categories of people such as the idolaters, the Jews, the Christians, and the Magians. The meaning is: do not look beyond the manifest and hidden blessings We have bestowed upon you toward what We have provided to a few pairs or categories of the disbelievers.

Perhaps some have taken His saying: "Do not extend your glance" (لا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ) as a metaphor for prolonging and sustaining the gaze. You know that the objective, in any case, is the prohibition of desire, inclination, and heart-attachment to the goods of life in their hands, such as wealth, power, and fame. What serves as a metaphor for that is the prohibition of the very act of looking at it, not just its prolongation. This is supported by what we will quote from the verse in Surah al-Kahf.

His saying: وَلا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ — and do not grieve for them (15:88); meaning, on account of their persistence in denial and mockery, and their insistence on not believing in you.
